core_meaning: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> literally means 'place' or 'spot', but its most important uses are grammatical: it freezes a verb in time ('just did', 'about to do', 'in the middle of doing') and it nominalizies clauses to mean 'the part where' or 'the fact that'. It is one of the most versatile words in Japanese and goes far beyond a physical location.

sentences.jp: ちょうど<ruby>家<rt>いえ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>出<rt>で</rt></ruby>るところだったのに、<ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>ってきた。
sentences.translation: I was just about to leave the house when it started raining.
sentences.notes: 〜るところだ expresses 'just about to do something' — here the past form ところだった adds a sense of 'bad timing'.

sentences.jp: あ、ちょうどよかった。<ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>から<ruby>説明<rt>せつめい</rt></ruby>するところだったんだ。
sentences.translation: Oh, perfect timing. I was just about to explain it.
sentences.notes: 〜するところだった in speech often carries a relieved or convenient nuance — 'you caught me right before I was going to…'.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>説明<rt>せつめい</rt></ruby>で<ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しいところは、この<ruby>部分<rt>ぶぶん</rt></ruby>だと<ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>います。
sentences.translation: The difficult part of her explanation, I think, is this section.
sentences.notes: <ruby>難<rt>むずか</rt></ruby>しいところ uses ところ as 'the part that is difficult' — a nominalizer turning an adjective clause into a noun phrase.

sentences.jp: <ruby>調査<rt>ちょうさ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>結果<rt>けっか</rt></ruby>を<ruby>分析<rt>ぶんせき</rt></ruby>しているところです。
sentences.translation: We are currently in the middle of analyzing the survey results.
sentences.notes: 〜しているところです is the polite way to say 'currently doing' — common in business or official contexts to explain an ongoing process.

sentences.jp: レポートをやっと<ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>き<ruby>終<rt>お</rt></ruby>わったところで、もう<ruby>夜中<rt>よなか</rt></ruby>の2<ruby>時<rt>じ</rt></ruby>だった。
sentences.translation: Right when I finally finished writing the report, it was already 2 in the morning.
sentences.notes: 〜たところで here means 'just at the point when something was completed' — ところ as a temporal anchor, not a physical place.

dont_use: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> should not be your default word for 'place' in concrete, physical contexts — <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> is more natural when you mean a physical location you can point to (e.g. 'a nice place to eat' is いい<ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby>, not いい<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> in most contexts). Also, don't use ところ when you want to say something is 'in progress' in a smooth, ongoing sense without a clear time anchor — in that case 〜ている alone is often more natural. Finally, avoid treating ところ as purely grammatical and forgetting it can also mean a person's home or location informally ('うちのところ' = 'at my place').

confused_with.contrast: Learners often say '<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きな<ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>こう' when talking about a specific physical destination, but '<ruby>好<rt>す</rt></ruby>きな<ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>行<rt>い</rt></ruby>こう' sounds more natural because <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby> is the go-to word for concrete, mappable locations. <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> is better for abstract 'places' in time, in a text, or in a situation, and for its many grammatical roles. When in doubt: if you can drop a pin on a map, use <ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby>.

confusion_set.use_when: Use when nominalizing a clause ('the part where', 'just about to', 'right in the middle of') or referring to an abstract place/point in time or text.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>今<rt>いま</rt></ruby>ちょうど<ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べ<ruby>終<rt>お</rt></ruby>わったところだよ。(I just finished eating right now.)

confusion_set.use_when: Use for a concrete, physical location — somewhere you can go to, point at, or describe geographically.
confusion_set.example: パーティーの<ruby>場所<rt>ばしょ</rt></ruby>はもう<ruby>決<rt>き</rt></ruby>まった？(Has the venue for the party been decided yet?)

confusion_set.use_when: Use for 'in the case of' or 'when a certain situation applies' — it marks a hypothetical or conditional circumstance, not a place.
confusion_set.example: <ruby>遅<rt>おく</rt></ruby>れる<ruby>場合<rt>ばあい</rt></ruby>は、<ruby>連絡<rt>れんらく</rt></ruby>してください。(If you're going to be late, please let us know.)

frequency: <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> is extremely common in both spoken and written Japanese. The grammatical uses (〜ているところ、〜たところ、〜るところ) appear constantly in daily speech and are essential for sounding natural at N3 and above.
anki_hint: Think of <ruby>所<rt>ところ</rt></ruby> as a 'point' — a point in space, a point in a text, or a point in time — which is why 'just about to', 'just finished', and 'the part where' all use the same word.
